speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Leopold Zylberman. This is tape 2. Leopold, you were talking about your schooling. How many years did you spend in the Tarbut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after, after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we went to high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, private. You see, and at that time, I was dropped a year back, because they were very strict on ages, et cetera. And it took a year, when [? Romak ?], my-- that means, was in the fourth form, and he wasn't too good a scholar. And times were bad, and that was after the family lost two kids, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two kids--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke earlier about your brother, who committed suicide--

speaker: interviewee
text: --suicide, and my daughter, who died in 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? Mina-- M-I-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died of pneumonia. And you see, it was a time where, you see, there were over-supply of intelligentsia. For Jews, it was very hard to get into the university. And the ones who finished, you see, at the university or law or something, there was no practice available, and hospitals, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it was very hard for Jews to get into university. Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it was that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they cared for their own people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the professors and the boards of the university. And there was talk, you see, that the Polish student-- the Jewish student had to be pretty good. And there was corruption and bribery, you see. That means the Vice Chancellor could get 10,000 in his pocket, you see. And there was an industry, you see, going among the Jews. But other than that, you had to be a brilliant student.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any open examples of antisemitism while you were in your later schooling years?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, it was, and it wasn't. You see, it was that we felt strange. You see, that we stuck to each own-- you see, more than that. And a few stupid remarks that you killed Jesus, or you see, about [INAUDIBLE], you see, [INAUDIBLE]. You see, about the blood for matzahs, you know. But you see, in a big town, we were mixed. You see, that means in the Hebrew School, you see, we never-- we were among the Jews, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your second school you went to, after the Tarbut, was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Jewish, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was Jewish-- did that school have a name, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: L'Or, L'Or-- L-A-- or L apostrophe O-R. It is 'to light' in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of your teacher or the headmaster at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was a Friedman, or something. It was a Jewish name, you see, because there was a lot of, of Jewish teachers, you see, Jewish nationals, you see, that means, teaching in Polish. So while Tarbut was a Hebrew school-- that means it is science and accounts and art or anything was in Hebrew, you see-- the other Jewish private high schools, they were taught Polish, other than two lessons in Hebrew, you see, as a second language, as a foreign language. There was a foreign language, French or German, and you had to pick one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one did you pick?

speaker: interviewee
text: French. So we, we didn't feel very much. You see, the question is that it was hard to get to university-- for Poles, too-- unless they had good marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend university?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, that means in '29-- you-- '28, '29-- you see, the thing was coming. They decided that Robin will go to a commercial college, which kept the top classes of high school with other-- you see, instead of Latin and maths, or something, there were emphasis of, accounting and correspondence and economic geography. You see, chemistry was switched over to merchandise quality, science, and it was a very good school. And a year later, I joined the same school, a class lower, and this was a very good school run by the Chamber of Commerce. And you had to have connections to get in, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have connections?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You see, actually my uncle, who died in New York--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Akiwa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-K-I-W-A. Akiwa, that means-- it's a Hebrew name. We called him Kuba. And he finished this school, and we helped, you see, that means, and I was a year after, was a very good school. You see, we were very well drilled in accountancy and merchandising and shopping, and sort of like the high schools or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years did you spend in this business college?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years. You see, and that means, the excursions, other than to the theater or exhibition, we spent a couple of days in, in banks, you know, and big factories. You see, we were shown machines and et cetera. And we spent the-- in the second and third year, spent a day on the stock exchange. And this was a school founded by the Chamber of Commerce, the Warsaw Chamber of Commerce, which was a Polish institution, but big first category businessmen could belong, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jewish people in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school, you see, that means, we started five Jews. We finished three, because the other went to work or apprenticeship, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many other in your class apart from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like 35 or 40, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we step back just a bit, here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just talk about-- if we could, about your Jewish upbringing, as you're growing up as a child in Warsaw? What sort of a religious upbringing did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: None, practically none. You see, that means we-- as I told you before, my parents were cheated. We knew Hebrew, but we didn't know the, the prayers, you see, the service routine-- you know, like in the cheders, in the religious school there. But we could speak Hebrew. And you see, on the New Year or atonement days-- you see that was the days that we had to go, you know, because we-- not to hurt the neighbors. You see, we could read, and that's all we read-- this and this. And until now, you see, I can read Hebrew. And I can, sort of, follow the cantor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- how often did you attend synagogue as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, you see, on holidays. You see, that means, as a child, you see, that means old people were attending, and we played football in the courtyard. You know-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a sort of a bar mitzvah, not, not the ones they're having here. But you see, that we went to-- went-- that means I didn't learn to the bar-- didn't have to learn to the bar mitzvah. They took me down to the schul, and the thing was telling me, and I was repeating, one after the other. You see, that means what was from the [INAUDIBLE] I could read, you know and what was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the holy book, you see. That means, I was repeating after the cantor. Repeat after me-- [HEBREW] et cetera. You know, it was a bit of a farce. I don't know, maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you see, because my father was in Moscow during the revolution, and he said that if I have seen things, you see, that means that God is not existing. And once we caught father, you see, smoking a cigar on Yom Kippur, you see, and having a feast, while we were playing football, and mama was sitting in the ladies' compartment, you know. And everything-- you can't smoke on Shabbos, but not on the balcony, so the bottom neighbors don't see you, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moving on, as far as a cultural life goes, was there much of a Jewish cultural life in Warsaw that you attended--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, you see, there were three newspapers in Yiddish and two newspapers in Polish, you see, edited and read by the Jews. There were a few magazines. Now, there was about two Yiddish theaters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever attend these theaters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the plays that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were sort of silly play, operettas, or something like this, you see. That means, we recently read memoirs of [INAUDIBLE], you see, and comedies, and historic plays, musicals and dramas, and very good translations. You see, now, the Jews took part, very big part, in Polish culture. There were poets, and there were painters, and there were writers. And you see, mostly, you see, during the school times, you see, we belonged to an organization. We would see that just like, you see, Poles didn't like the Jews, and Jews didn't like the Poles-- so among the Jews, the religious ones, the orthodox, didn't like the progressive. The progressive didn't like orthodox. Now, among the progressive, you see, there were Zionists, and there were Bundist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any of these youth groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, we subscribed to Hashomer Hatzair that were left-- Zionists, you see, labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember attending meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, we were attending meetings. We were, [? Robak ?] and I, we were in a special group that spoke Hebrew. And the other, you see, sort of platoons, or some-- There was a platoon, and there was a [INAUDIBLE] We started as, you see, sort of sparrows-- the group of sparrows, and then we got, got promoted to cubs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What activities did you partake?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What activities did you do with this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were having discussions.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what?

speaker: interviewee
text: About Israel, about Jews, about history. And now, there was a Jewish holiday called Lag B'Omer, and so we went to excursions, and we had sort of Purim concerts, all the six Tarbut schools had the circus. There was a choir, and there was aerobics, gymnastics, and all sorts of things. And I was sitting behind the choir and just, on a very low voice, you see, repeated. And now, when there was Lag B'Omer, we used to go, you see, by steamship, you see, to the-- to a place more up the river to [INAUDIBLE] and put tents, and play the bow and arrow-- most kids do. It was sort of scouts.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a result of what you'd learnt going to this youth-- Zionist youth group, did you have any plans or dreams of going to Israel yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, it wasn't crystallized. I sort of-- I felt very well, and I felt all right in Warsaw. You see, that means when I finished college, you see friends from [? Harrods ?] asked me to help them in the bookkeeping. And I was running the bookkeeping, the illegal for-- bookkeeping for the false aliyah. Because they were, you see, at the time, after the White Paper, you see, they wouldn't let Jews into Palestine, and there was organizing you see, smuggling them through Turkey. I was helping with accounts, but not active, not actively.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this that you were helping with these books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this that you're talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was between '35, '38 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this period--

speaker: interviewer
text: In this period, it was Hitler's rise to power. And in that period, '35 to '38, did you notice any changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, that means the Poles, you see, they were tolerant, you see. That means they keep to their own, just like we kept to our own. Say, a Polish nobleman, a gentleman liked rather intelligent Jew's company against a peasant, you know. Now, you see, the Polish, politically, they had a national party, a moderate party and the labor party, you see, [INAUDIBLE] or something. They were hating each other, of course. And you see, that means there was the nationals had a newspaper, you see, with a few articles against Jews. They didn't like, you see, the question of they can cover economies, or something like this. Now, the other Polish papers were neutral, but they were not sympathetic, but neutral, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at school that means when we went to the commercial school, we were among Poles, and they hated every other Jew, except the boys in the class, you know? The boys in the class, they say, you Jews do this and that and that, but you're all right, you're one of us, you know. They said [INAUDIBLE]. What did you think of that attitude that they had?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought, a matter of fact, to be friendly, frank. You see, I just took it as it was. You know, that means by-- I finished school before Hitler came to power. And they made a lot of jokes about Hitler and things, and then that he was a painter, and what it means, heil, Hitler. And they said, well, he was a painter, so up to here, oil paint, other, you'd see here's rubber, it's plastic. There were revues, see, at the Sunday [INAUDIBLE] were making jokes about Hitler, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the attitude, what was the general attitude--

speaker: interviewee
text: -- that before he went into-- came to power, and even after, you see-- you see very-- a few caricatures in the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you describe the attitude of these people towards Hitler at this point? Did they take him seriously?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, they didn't. But later on, you see, there was-- I don't know whether you heard about it-- Poland became independent in 1918. After the Bolshevik War, there was the first election, and then the right wing took over government. And they put everything, all the heroes who fought for independence, aside. And in 1926, Marshal Pilsudski, you see, took over. You see, he-- part of the army was for him-- you see, mostly central and south, while the west were rightists, et cetera. And there was a few days' revolution. I remember, you see, there was shooting and so on. And then Pilsudski went-- took, took over, and he kept the job of Defense Minister, but he was running the show. There was a Premier and a Foreign Minister, but Pilsudski was running the show. And during his time, it was very good. He was very liberal. He was himself delivering speeches, criticizing. You see, he just could say that the Parliament is a brothel, and something like this. You see, he died in 1935 or 1936, you know. And then, in-- within the government, started the-- you see, and then Hitler was already in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what changes did you experience yourself after Hitler came to power in that period of 1935 to '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally not, because you see, I was working in a firm that sold national products.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that firm?

speaker: interviewee
text: The firm was Omtax. And another-- it worked under two, under two names-- Omtax and Kolux. You see, there is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-M-T-A-X Proprietary Limited, and then we-- Kolux-- K-O-L-U-X, just like [INAUDIBLE] looks, looks. So it was all-- you see, when I finished school, you know, that means it was very hard to get jobs. I eventually got a job as a bookkeeper, a correspondent, and messenger boy, money collector all in one. And then my boss went broke, and we went to Israel, and I was without a job. And my brother, brother recommend me to one of his competitors, who had the agency of [INAUDIBLE]. And he was just about going broke. And I helped my brother with his two partners, you see, to buy him off and keep the agency of [INAUDIBLE]. So that means in 1936, I got myself an executive position, because I was replacing the import manager and the chief accountant. And, and at that time, I started writing my own salary, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that you experienced no personal incidents of antisemitism. What was going on in the streets around you where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means nothing special. You see, the thing was we had, from the papers, in the [INAUDIBLE], you see. And then in certain streets were fights, you know. But there was a district, you see, where there were wholesale-- I can't remember, [INAUDIBLE]-- wholesale fruit, you know, business. And there were very strong fellows, you see, porters, et cetera. And whenever there was a trouble, they were calling back, because the Bund has its own fighting group. Well, small trouble, you see, they beat the fellow up in the street and ran away. You see, that means you couldn't help it-- simply hooliganism. But, you see, where there was bigger trouble, they called up the thing, and those strong fellows, you see-- you see, at the time, there were no trucks, and there were no forklifts. You see, they were carrying burdens on, on their backs. And they were coming and helping out. So in Warsaw, it was steady. Now, after Pilsudski's death-- '36, '37, '38-- they changed the Constitution. They changed the way of voting to reduce the, the number of minorities. You see, because before that, there was a group of minorities-- for the Jews, the Ukrainians, et cetera. And the chief of the group was the Deputy Greenbaum. And he was talking loud in the Parliament. But you see, when they started fighting, they changed everything. And at the time, you see, just in '38, they introduced concentration camps, where they could keep, without sentencings, you see-- sort of a Dachau. And for instance, if you were not a registered company, and you wanted to call yourself-- eh, Roseflower, or something-- a company, a private company-- you had to put you name. And the Jewish name were Jewish. You see, that we saw Moshe Chaim, or something. That means you have to say-- you see [INAUDIBLE] Repair Course-- eh, owners, Chaim, [INAUDIBLE] and Moshe, others, so, for argument's sake. Well, we could say that we could make a company, you see, to prevent this. But you see, that means if you didn't-- were not a registered company-- it was hard to register. It was expensive, too. You see, you have to put if you sold a bar of soap, it had to be printed-- manufactured by Rose Soap, owners, Rubin, [? Zylberlichten ?]-- eh, you know, this sort of business. And it was the Minister, the Prime Minister said that don't that we not hooligans, we're not fighting. But the way, if you don't like Jews, you can boycott, you see. That means fighting is not allowed, but the boycott of Jewish business is OK. And that was in '37, 8, 9. In that way, they started Polish-- you see, that means Polish firm. And that was OK, OK firm. You see, boycotts OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to take a break here and change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
